experiments.title: Does adding animation to a sticky bar increase donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that animating the sticky bar for Giving Tuesday will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having the animation will draw eyes to the sticky bar and having the message rotate leans into having more value proposition of why a donor should donate on the element itself.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/14/2025
Each year KCBI utilizes sticky bars for a Giving Tuesday direct ask. In the past we’ve tested into different elements to add to it and what copy should be used to make it most effective. Last year we learned that having a specific goal for Giving Tuesday to set it apart from CYE was helpful in getting people to convert. This year we wanted to see if animating the copy text to show different value propositions instead of just one could potentially increase the donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that animating the sticky bar for Giving Tuesday resulted in a significant increase in donor conversion rate compared to a static sticky bar. The treatment showed a 64.9% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that the animation drew more attention and potentially communicated more value propositions to donors.

It's not recommended to use this tactic as the 'normal' moving forward, but it is something to consider using for a short period of time as the novelty effect will wear off.

experiments.title: Will adding a testiony on the doantion page influence people to give?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimony for the campaign donation pages will achieve a higher number of transactions because the testimony will show social proof, that other people donate, and share the reasons for it.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
For CYE this year we wanted to see if we could increase the number of transactions on the campaign giving page by adding in a donor testimonial. The goal of this test is to if a donor testimony is more distracting to viewers, or sparks the reason to give even more.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor testimonial to the campaign donation pages significantly increased the number of transactions. The treatment group, which included the donor testimonial, saw a 615.6% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 96.9%.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the effectiveness of donor testimonials in increasing donations. This outcome suggests that donor testimonials can be a powerful tool in encouraging others to give, as they reinforce the reasons why someone should donate. It would be beneficial to further explore different types of testimonials, placement on the page, and messaging to optimize the impact on donation numbers.

Additionally, these findings highlight the importance of incorporating donor perspectives and stories into fundraising efforts. By showcasing the impact of donations through testimonials, nonprofits like KCBI can create a more compelling case for support and connect donors to the mission on a more personal level. This can ultimately lead to increased engagement, loyalty, and support for the organization's work.

experiments.title: How adding an additional acquisition form field adds friction
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional form field for acquistion offers will achieve a lower conversion rate and a higher cost per email because adding an additional field presents another opportunity to add friction to the page, which is another place for users to abandon the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2024 - 05/24/2024
KCBI just launched a new radio station in Waco. Which lead to several brainstorming sessions trying to figure out how we can separate a DFW or general states listener from a Waco listener from the point of acquisition.
For this test, we wanted to see if adding a zip code form field on an acquisition offer could gain more information about our users, while not sacrificing too much cost on the front end.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an additional form field for acquisition offers led to a significant decrease in email acquisition, with a 43.5% decrease in conversion rate for all traffic. This confirms the hypothesis that adding an extra field can increase friction on the page, causing more users to abandon the process.

Not only did it decrease conversion rate on the page, it increased the cost to acquire a new name by 57%.

experiments.title: Will Adding a Reader’s Favorite Message to a Content Offer Landing Page Help with Sign Up Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a “Reader’s Favorite flag for visitors on the Romans in Action landing page will achieve higher sign up conversion rates because visitors will see that this is a popular eBook through a visual queue. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2024 - 03/28/2024
We saw that the sign-up rates on the KCBI content offer sign-up pages could be higher so we could ultimately present more opportunities for visitors to share their email addresses and become donors. We were interested in trying a couple of visual queues based on the content offer performance to let our landing page visitors know, this is a a “fan favorite” of the KCBI audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a "Reader's Favorite" flag on the landing page for the Romans in Action content offer did not result in higher sign-up conversion rates. In fact, the treatment actually saw a decrease of 21.3% in email acquisition compared to the control group. This indicates that the visual queue did not have the intended effect on visitors.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of visual cues and ensure that they align with the preferences and behaviors of the target audience. It may be beneficial to conduct surveys or gather feedback from visitors to understand what factors would make them more likely to sign up for content offers.

Additionally, it is essential to continue testing different strategies and elements on landing pages to identify what resonates best with visitors. By continuously experimenting and analyzing the results, KCBI can optimize their campaigns and improve sign-up conversion rates in the future.

experiments.title: How stating a specific goal in the headline of a pop-up for CYE fundraising affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the goal in the pop-up headline for website visitors will achieve a greater conversion rate of visitors to donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/23/2022
Our CYE fundraising strategy included a pop-up on the Libertas homepage with the goal of delivering the Tuttle Twins series to 200 public schools by raising $50,000. Most visitors to the site want to ensure that we are teaching children the value of liberty so we appealed to this desire with the headline “Help teach Kids the Value of Liberty by Making a Year-End Donation” which is clear and direct. However, we also know that a tangible goal appeals to donors, so we wanted to see if we stated the goal in the headline if it would lift response.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment pop-up saw a 4.3% lift in donor conversion rate with a 98.7% level of confidence. This experiment is only based on 6 total gifts, but due to this campaign being very time sensitive and the control pop-up receiving 0 gifts, we decided to roll out the treatment headline to 100% of traffic.

We believe the treatment headline won in this experiment because the goal gave donors something tangible to give to instead of a more broad topic like "teaching kids about liberty".

experiments.title: How an organic looking Facebook Ad impacts ad engagement and email acquisition in a content offer funnel
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an organic looking ad design will increase the number of emails acquired by increasing engagement with the ad.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2023 - 05/30/2023
KCBI is a Christian broadcast ministry in Dallas Texas. For their latest devotional eBook content offer, they wanted to test a more “authentic” and “organic” looking design to their ads. They hypothesized that a plain image with text against a solid colored background, mimicking Facebook’s status update template, would increase engagement on the ad because it wouldn’t look like an “ad” compared to their standard design that featured the eBook cover in the image and ad copy describing the resource. The only copy in the treatment would be the copy in the organic looking image.
They set it up as a AB test in Facebook and ran it for 10 days with equal budget behind each variation
experiments.keyLearnings:
The organic design had had a significantly lower CPM of just $7.55 vs $20.75 on the control. However, the Link Click Through Rate on the treatment was significantly lower than the CTR on the control. Just 45% on the treatment vs 78% on the control. This meant that the treatment had a smaller lead in cost per link click.  $0.96 on the treatment vs $1.38 on the control.
Ultimately, we were trying to get more subscribers, not just clicks. The treatment had a 90% lower subscriber rate which led to a $10.23 gross cost per name for the organic design vs a $2.37 gross cost per name for the control.
What this shows is that we don't just need more clicks, we need the right clicks on our ads and the lack of additional value propositional copy in the ad led to a significantly higher proportion of unmotivated traffic to the landing page. Additional testing is needed to see if an "organic" ad design with ad copy would perform better than our standard ad creative.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy statement below the submit button on content offer landing page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a privacy statement below the submit button for landing page visitors will achieve greater email conversion because it will decrease anxiety about data security and increase the appeal of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2022 - 09/30/2022
For a faith-based media organization, we wanted to know how including a privacy statement below the form submission button would affect email acquisition rates. We know that privacy continues to be a growing priority for users online and we wanted to test whether or not anticipating any reluctance to submit payment information online at the point of requesting a devotional eBook would reduce anxiety and encourage more people to complete the form.
The privacy statement below the button on the treatment reads: “*We take data and privacy seriously. Your information will be kept safe and will be used to send you your eBook. “
~50% of traffic received the control and ~50% of traffic received the treatment.  We set this up in Unbounce and, unfortunately, didn’t reset the conversions report so the samples look skewed in the report below as “control” reflects all samples since the launch of the content offer not just the ones during the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Based on the results from this Unbounce experiment, it's clear that including a privacy statement below the form submission button had a major impact on email acquisition rates.
This shows that anticipating privacy concerns in our customers and adapting our strategies to address those concerns can have a substantive impact on our overall marketing efforts. In the future, it will be valuable to continue testing our privacy statements to determine what language is most effective in communicating security, understanding that our customers expect transparency and taking responsible steps to secure their personal data. We will be testing this approach on donation pages as well.
Unfortunately, we were not able to validate a similar increase in downstream donor conversion from this test.

experiments.title: How making the organization the actor of a Giving Tuesday donation page value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making KCBI the hero of the value proposition on the Giving Tuesday donation page will decrease donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2022 - 11/29/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. They wanted to see if donors were more motivated to give  TO KCBI because of what KCBI would do with their gift or if they would be motivated to give because of what their GIFT would do THROUGH KCBI. So, which “hero” motivated more visitors to the donation page on Giving Tuesday to become donors.
Historically, they’ve tried to always emphasize the donor as the hero in their donation pages.
For the control, they emphasized in the headline and body copy the impact the donor would have by giving. In the treatment, they tweaked it slightly to emphasize how the impact is through KCBI. Examples include….
“You can give practical help and biblical truth this Giving Tuesday” vs “Give Practical Help and Biblical Truth this Giving Tuesday Through KCBI“
“By making a gift right now, you will be broadcasting the sounds of hope and the Good News of Jesus, as well as ensuring that practical help and biblical truth is carried out through our community…”
vs
“By making a gift to KCBI right now, you will help us broadcast the sounds of hope and the Good News of Jesus, as well as ensure that we provide practical help and biblical truth to our community…”
“Will you give right now?” vs “Will you give to KCBI right now?”
“Please step forward and make your special Giving Tuesday gift using the secure form below. You will have an eternal impact on the lives of others.”
vs
“Please step forward and make your special Giving Tuesday gift to KCBI using the secure form below. Your gift will enable us to have an eternal impact on the lives of others.”
We split the traffic 50/50 and ran it during their Giving Tuesday campaign that began a week early online. We hypothesized that the control page with the donor as the hero of the value proposition would have the greater conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
To our surprise, the control donation page that made the donor the hero (actor) in the value proposition had a decreased donor conversion rate and our treatment making KCBI the hero of the page increased conversion by 31%. When looking at the traffic by segment, all segments favored the treatment over the control.  In our analysis of the results, we looked to see if there were any large donations that may be skewing revenue and average gift results and found only a total of five gifts. Removing these gave us the following results:
Control (donor as hero):
98 donations, 24.7% conversion rate, $8,089 revenue, and $82.54 average gift
Treatment (KCBI as hero)
126 donations, $9,003.85 revenue, and $71.45 average gift.
This analysis did not change the level of confidence or the overall result. We still brought in approximately $1,000 in revenue on the treatment despite the lower average gift thanks to the higher donor conversion rate.
While our thinking has been that donors want to be the actor/hero, it is possible that the way KCBI emphasized this in their control body copy made it sound like the donor was doing "work." The donor could have anxiety about "saving lives" "sharing the Gospel" etc. The treatment page made it clear that KCBI did these things through the donor's gift.
More testing is needed to see the influence of a value proposition with KCBI as the hero outside of a unique campaign like Giving Tuesday. It is possible that the unique motivations of this day of giving influenced the results.

experiments.title: How asking for $10 instead of $50 on a content offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for $10 instead of $50 on a content offer donation page will reduce mental friction and increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/11/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. They use free content offers to acquire donors online. They wanted to see if they could improve donor response by asking for less on their instant-donation page that loads after someone completes the form to claim a free eBook. Their offer donation pages all had giving arrays that started at $50. They hypothesized that $50 could seem like a lot of money to give after getting a free eBook, especially during a period of high inflation like what the US was experiencing. They wanted to see if asking for $10 would reduce mental friction and increase donor conversion.
They created a new version of the donation page for their Praying Scriptures for Your Child devotional eBook and split the traffic 50/50. They ran the experiment for one month and saw the following results.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 4 weeks, we did not have greater than 50 gifts in the sample size, but we did validate a significant lift on donor conversion. We believe that, during periods of high inflation like we are experiencing right now, asking for less, especially in acquisition offers, reduces mental friction and makes it easier for someone to give their first gift. We will continue to test this in their future offers when inflation cools down to see if we can ask for a higher gift amount without harming our donor acquisition rates.

We did however show that donors are much more likely to click through on an appeal if asked for less. Further testing is needed to see if we can also improve donor conversion with this approach.

experiments.title: How communicating the impact of paying for credit card fees on a donation page influenced donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that communicating trust and goodwill towards the donor and clarifying the impact of covering credit card fees on the donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2022 - 06/10/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. For their main donation page, they wanted to test the impact of asking donors to cover the credit card fees associated with their online gift.
The first test they performed was a test of removing the option to pay for fees altogether. That test resulted in 20% decrease in donor conversion with a 82% level of confidence. Our hypothesis was that having an option to cover fees on their donation page may have been giving donors more clarity about what their gift was going towards and thus removing it altogether reduced donor conversion. As a result of this test, they decided they wanted to test the language used on the page to explain the value impact of covering credit card fees. Our hypothesis was that by communicating trust and appreciation of the donor’s desire to have a greater impact with their gift, we could cause more to choose to cover the fee and increase donor conversion. Their current fees option had a vague promise that covering fees would, “maximize your donation.” Our variation instead clarified the impact of covering fees on the outreaches donor’s cared about (giving bibles, providing ultrasounds, biblical resources, etc) and recognized the donors for their desire to do those very things.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment ran during and after a major high urgency campaign which muddied the results of the test. On no traffic segment did the new fee language improve conversion. We may test this again in the future to be sure, but for now we will continue with offering the option to cover credit card fees so that donors can "maximize their donation." It is possible that the greater length of the treatment distracted donors by adding friction.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly donate button to the main navigation menu impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly donate button to the main navigation menu will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2022 - 06/10/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. They wanted to see if they could improve donor conversion on their website by adding a monthly donation button to their main navigation next to their one-time donate button. Monthly donors are an important part of their program and they hoped that this additional callout would increase monthly donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Not only did the addition of a monthly donate button increase donor conversion, but it increased revenue as well by 138.3% with a 94% level of confidence. Our hypothesis is that donors appreciated the clarity of knowing the two types of donations they could make at the start of the process while also just simply giving them another visual reminder of how easy it is to donate. While we originally hoped to also validate an increase in monthly donor conversion, the small sample size of monthly donations was not sufficient for a reliable level of confidence.
Caveats. The language in the onetime donation option changed from the control to the treatment. This muddied the results of the test. Also by adding an additional button, we had to take the onetime gift button out of the nav menu with the other menu items and present it on its own.
Future Testing:
We need to ensure that the changes to the nav menu are rolled out to the entire KCBI site to increase sample size and gain more accurate data. With that in place, we can test the following elements from this test to learn what had the greatest  impact:
Test position of the onetime gift buttonTest the copy on the onetime gift button (give vs donate)test adding a monthly donate button again once we have the data from the prior two tests.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing subscribers with a sample of a content offer in the promo email will increase clicks to the offer signup page.

KCBI is a leading Christian radio station and ministry in Dallas, Texas.  About a year ago, they created a “verse a day” subscription offer for their subscribers. People who enrolled received a verse or two in their inboxes each morning. They wanted to promote the offer to their file again but wanted to see if this time they could get more clicks to the landing page by providing subscribers with a sample verse like the ones they would get. This increased the appeal and clarity of the offer and led to more visits to the signup page. We will continue to test into ways of allowing people to "try before they buy" so we can reduce the mental friction that comes from not knowing what an offer will be like.

experiments.title: How asking for less impacted year end giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for less than their previous highest amount during a year end fundraising campaign will increase overall revenue and response.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2021 - 12/07/2021
During the beginning of their year end campaign, KCBI decided to see if they could statistically validate a concept they had stumbled upon during a previous high urgency campaign. The idea was that if we asked existing donors for less than their previous highest gift amount and gave them a reason for doing so, we could inspire more people to give overall.
Originally, our belief was that it may lower average gift but would increase response rate enough to improve overall revenue. However, we found that it was able to increase both average gift and response rate for the segment but did not reach the 95% level of statistical validity we look for when determining a winner. With this in mind, we tested the same concept again at the start of their year end campaign.
For this test, we divided the file between control and treatment. For the control, we used email personalization to ask them for a specific amount (based upon a previous highest gift amount) and identified that we’re looking for a specific number of donors. For example, if a donor had given $20 in the past, we would ask: “Would you consider being one of 50 donors to make a gift of $25 right now.”
For the treatment, we used the same logic but asked them for a level down compared to the control. For example, if a donor had given $20, we would ask them to be one of 50 donors to make a $10 gift right now.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
After sending three separate emails to the distinct segments using similar language in each language and maintaining the same control / treatment split through the entire series, we discovered that the treatment group saw a 193.9% increase to overall revenue. This came from increasing conversion rate by 49% and average gift by 97%.
When digging into the results, we found that the biggest revenue increase came from donors giving between $250 and $500. The treatment group had eight donors giving in that range while the control had none.
It should also be noted that we removed a $2,600 gift from the results since it was such a large outlier when compared to the other gifts. When it is included, we observed a 39% increase with the treatment with a 73% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How including a first name personalization in the subject line of a recurring donor cultivation email affects open rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Personalizing the subject line for a more one-to-one communication will increase open rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2021 - 02/11/2021
KCBI was sending out a cultivation emails to their recurring donors, letting them know all of the ways their recurring gifts were going to change lives this year. We wanted to test a personalized subject line that included the donor name to see how that affected the open rate, because one of our goals is to increase cultivation for their monthly donors to continue to increase their already excellent recurring donor retention rate. We split the email by active and inactive emails (hadn’t opened the last five emails), so that we could see the affects on engaged audience versus unengaged.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In the inactive segment, the personalization made a big impact in opens, increasing the number of folks opening by 54%. Our hypothesis that personalization would increase email reactivation through a more one-to-one communication proved to be true.
The active segment only saw a small increase in open rate, not enough to be statistically valid. We believe this is because this is an already very engaged audience, as recurring donors who regularly open emails.

experiments.title: How the length of a pledge impacts email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the length of a pledge/petition have a difference on the conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/15/2020
In an effort to acquire email addresses, KCBI created a pledge/petition for people to sign that aligned people’s beliefs with a core value of theirs. The original pledge had ten statements of belief within it and we wondered if the number of beliefs statements would impact conversion.
Ultimately what we were trying to figure out is this, can a pledge have too many belief statements resulting in friction and therefore drop-offs?
We cut the number of belief statements from 10 to 5 for the treatment. We chose the 5 for the treatment based on what we thought were the most “consumable” for people – they weren’t lengthy, weighted, and seemed easiest to understand, read, and align with.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we ended up seeing was a 9.3% increase in conversion for the shorter pledge! Our biggest takeaway here was that less is sometimes more. The pledge with 5 belief statements converted at a higher rate than the one with 10. This could be based on a couple of things…1. The length of the pledge2. Simplicity of the statements of beliefThe next step would be to look at the language and wording of each statement and begin to test there.
Now that we have seen that less is more we can begin iterating on what words or phrasing should be used.

experiments.keyLearnings: It does seem like Facebook may have favored one ad over the other. In the future I would like to run this same test from the beginning and potentially create a treatment landing page which also includes the iPad image in order to increase consistency for the user.
